caption:
‘I tend to break down when I make a record. You have to destroy the versions of yourself that provide too easy a solution. Someone comes along in yourself, and he has a slogan, he has a view on love, a position on the world... To murder all those false persons that arise and try to tell you what the song is, to get to that place where you can defend every word... that takes a slaughter. I write to murder the selves that whisper untruths to me.’ – Leonard Cohen
 
Lately, I find myself drawn to Cohen’s art, not only in terms of his music – which has been endlessly dissected – but his writing process which holds much inspiration for those seeking.
 
At their most tender, Cohen’s musings were spiritual, solemn – such reverence and warmth were not reserved for a higher form of consciousness, but extended to his lovers, and equally to the lovelorn and the broken. He wrote of years spent alone with oneself, a graceful balance of meditation and yearning. It would be reductive to call his music melancholic because he celebrated so much of the beauty that hides behind the veil of melancholy, he conveyed ways to act with grace in these moments of suffering. 
 
At their darkest, the songs questioned – with sharpness and precision – the very form of consciousness that he prayed to, immortalized. This was a cynical, whiskey-soaked side to him, often conveyed with a gruff baritone. You could almost picture him in a suit, smoking a cigar at some local bar, contemplating his place in the world.
‘If you are the dealer, let me out of the game. If you are the healer, it means I am broken and lame.’ he sings in ‘You Want it Darker’, a song from his final album of the same name, just weeks before his death.
 
The music arose from his relentless devotion to his craft. ‘A song will yield if you stick with it long enough’, he once said, before going on to warn, ‘but long enough is way beyond any reasonable duration’. A sense of seriousness, of weighing each word with care, permeates through his work.
(contd. in comments)
#leonardcohen #musicreview #singersongwriter #folkmusic #indiefolk #chamberfolk #poetry #songwriting #songwriter #writingtips

Patti Smith is like one of those friends that bombard you with references to obscure poets and authors when you talk with them, not to appear knowledgeable but because they are genuinely passionate about art. Reading this book felt like having a conversation, one in which she casually says things like 'So there I was, hanging out with my dear friend William S. Burroughs'. 

I didn't know Patti as a torchbearer for the Beat generation, but the book is also fascinating from that viewpoint (she's the only female Beat poet I know of). A lot of 'M Train' is stream-of-consciousness if one is being kind and scatterbrained if one is not, so it's perhaps not for everyone. But if you follow her down the rabbit hole, where she jumps from talking about Murakami, to a dream she had last night, to the repetitive and meditative rituals of a writer, to her interest in (what some might consider to be mundane) American detective shows, you will be entertained and at the very least, have enough book/poetry recommendations to last you a couple of years.

#pattismith #bookstagram #mtrain #surreal #surrealism #beatgeneration #poetry #beatpoetry

Saw this in a second hand bookstore in Berlin and I identified the author's name as it was mentioned in a song I loved - 'the past is a grotesque animal' by of Montreal, an 11 minute long psychedelic dance romp about the narrators urge to fuck things up, and a relentless examination of their relationships in the past.

Bataille takes this investigation up a notch - the prose is gorgeous, but so, so depraved. It's a fever dream of a novella, one that is over before you have the time to process it properly.

Oh and the song is worth a listen as well, here's a lyric that connects it nicely to the book:
'It's like we weren't made for this world, though I wouldn't really want to meet someone who was.'
#georgesbataille #frenchliterature #ofmontreal #penguinbooks

Fiction review - ‘Kafka on The Shore’ by Haruki Murakami. Murakami books are movements in themselves. Kafka is a catalyst for change, very much like music by the Archduke trio the book mentions, something which when experienced can make you a different person. There's so many recurring themes and self references, such vivid symbolism - the crows, the butterfly (one of the best lines in the book likens writing the song 'Kafka on the Shore' to holding a butterfly by the wings without disturbing its motion), the distant room where you can find chords, the ancient Greek legend about us being only half of a whole and likening this to the main characters only having half a shadow, the repetitive reminders to conquer your fear and anger, and of everyone needing a place they can come back to. The images are repeated at various points throughout, pounding their way into your head eventually. 
The book is chaotic, lacks order and can get somewhat scatterbrained, but this works out in its favor in a way. A uniting theme, if you're looking for one, is how some people need to go to the farthest edges of sanity to better fit in the world around them. Like any decent Murakami though, it can be tough to get through, and is not a book to be read in one sitting. It is, however, a book to pick up when you have time, and unwrap slowly. The more you think of it, the more you can unravel... there is always something new to be found, something that kept me coming back. Very much like Kafka, I'm not sure if I consciously learnt anything from the ordeal, but the journey, in a way, changed my perspective on the self. Again, words don't really do it justice. As Nakata says, you have to get there to understand it.

Fiction review - ‘Never Let Me Go’ by Kazuo Ishiguro. 
I remember watching the TV with my grandmother as a kid. In retrospect, quite a bit of what we saw used to be shallow and only mildly entertaining, but I stuck around because I got to spend time with her. Anyways, a vivid memory of those times (I would've been six years old) is of us watching this show where the protagonist dies. And I hadn't got the intricacies of death nailed down just yet, so I prodded the truth out of my parents. It left me scared to imagine that one day, I too would die - it was a simple feeling of dread, nothing more, nothing less.

The reason I mention this is, I found myself recounting that memory as I got done with the book... It has that air of death and gangrene to it, and can often come across as gloomy and desolate. 
I loved the way the first two parts of the book were written; the idyllic atmosphere of Hailsham are so well described. There is a certain warmth and happiness to these pages, although certain incidents do point to how Ishiguro is going to take your feelings, step all over them and make it hurt just enough for you to recover, and repeat this until the end whence a sharp and short blow suffices. (i got carried away a bit here) (contd.)

Album Appreciation Post
So Anupama (@puumzu) wrote a really fun piece about one of her (and my) favorites, The Cure's 'Disintegration'! Disintegration is arguably one of the best albums of the 80s, if not all time, quite simply because, as strange and eerie it may be at some points, there's hardly a single moment you can't want to put yourself in Robert's shoes and think of what on Earth made him write these songs. It can make you believe that the song that you're listening to comes from exactly what it sounds like but if you listen close enough, you won't ever be able to understand what it really means, or maybe, you won't really want to know what led to them. But this album is a masterpiece in the way it sounds, the feel it carries, the places it takes you to and that every time you listen to it, it coughs up an entirely new interpretation in your mind.
The album begins with Plainsong, a song that I would imagine plays when you're being carried into heaven after you've died. And right after it throws you into Pictures Of You, which is a beautifully sad song, possibly about regrets with a past love interest or maybe even an obsessive necrophilic person. And that's precisely why The Cure is so brilliant; you just never know with them! There's also tracks like Lovesong which has got to be one of my favourite love songs, even though there is a lingering The Cure-esque creeps in this track too. I believe this song was written by Robert for his wife at the time though, so I'm sure the creeps were all with good intent. There isn't a single song on this album that I don't like and there are very few albums for which I feel that way. Right from start to finish.
Seriously though, how can songs as creepy as Lullaby, as gripping as Fascination Street, as romantic as Lovesong, as majestic as Plainsong and as wonderfully ordinary as Untitled be put together in one album? It's The Cure at their peak, don't think so much, just go wherever the music takes you and be grateful it's there at all. #postpunkrevival #howeverfaraway #iwillalwaysloveyou

Album Appreciation Post 
#indie #indierock #alternative #folk #indiefolk #singersongwriter #arcadefire #alternativemusic #instamusic #guitar #music
(By @musicwilluntunethesky)
On my first listen, I didn’t see Funeral as anything special or even cathartic. At that time it felt like a collection of standard indie rock, 4/4 beats and simple guitar strums. 
I kept revisiting it on and off throughout college (at the suggestion of certain people) and grew to love little moments initially. Going for a walk at 6 in the morning to ‘Tunnels’ was a wonderful experience – the violins and guitars keep the momentum and you don’t feel like stopping. Speaking of ‘Tunnels’, what a track! It is suited for those moments of change – early morning revelations about yourself and your path ahead, for welcoming the winter, for your first love. And vocalist Win Butler has a poetic flair to the way he writes. ‘You change all the lead sleeping in my head to gold’, he sings. It is the sound of youth, the good with the bad – the wonder, the romance, the anxiety and the uncertainty of youth condensed into songs that you can shout at the top of your voice to. Being about youth, there are also parts when the singer realizes that growing up is inevitable; as we grow older we tend to face loss in ways we were shielded from before - ‘My family tree’s losing all its leaves’, Regine sings on ‘Learning to Drive’. Some songs act as a call to arms, bringing to notice all that has been lost in the process of growing up – optimism, aggression, for some even vitality; ‘Now that I’m older, my heart’s colder and I can see that it’s a lie’. I had this theory of firsts. I think that there’s a lot of joys in (the later stages of?) youth that you get to experience for the first time. Such as your first kiss, first Murakami novel, first Radiohead album. First long run, first time performing at a concert, first house party. These firsts are what keep life fresh and new, keep the wonder alive. But I fear that as I grow old, these firsts will slow down in how frequently they appear; after all, there are only so many new experiences that life has to offer. (cont'd in comments)

Album Appreciation Post 
#radiohead #indie#alternative #psychedelia #acid #tripping #indierock #instamusic #psychedelic 
This album review is for In Rainbows by Radiohead. Written (and illustrated) by Jai (@newpearlgarden), its something I could relate to in a massive way, being a big fan of the band myself.
'Radiohead and I have a very old history. The first Radiohead song that I heard was house of cards and I was all of 12. I couldn't relate to it at all. The distorted, wavy figures and RBG patterns in the music video weirded me out. I loved pop music back then. Then at 14 I heard my second Radiohead song, 'Lotus Flower'. Again, I was intrigued but not enough. I remember trying to listen to them after this really attractive senior from my school said that they were very good. I don't know where she is now. God I had a huge crush on her. Then I turned 17 and it was the time of my experimentation with a lot of drugs. When I dropped acid for the first time, I didn't know what music should I listen to. Next day I went and asked this guy about it. He suggested Radiohead. I dropped acid again at 2 PM in the Delhi heat, traveling in an auto rickshaw. It felt like the movie Tron but with a lot of bright yellow light. Everything became florescent. The patterns were overwhelming and I decided to watch the house of cards video again. Boy did it make sense. After that I heard the entire album, while sitting in a park in Munirka Enclave. My math class was at 5 and I just wanted to peak before I went.

Right from 15 Step to weird fishes to All I need, each song's a journey. In the sky I could see all kinds of manifestations of clouds. When 'Videotape' came, I saw an old CRT TV with a VCR player, the screen showing my childhood in a very kaleidoscopic way. It wasn't there, yet I could see it. Again, acid trips are very personal. This album which had a huge impact on me; I was getting over my first break up and I was in class 12th and in that state I was experimenting with LSD. In Rainbows healed me and helped me develop a taste in experimental music. It was the zenith for me; I couldn't appreciate any other rock act for a while. Can you find a better act than them?'

Album appreciation post #progrock #prog #instamusic #rock #stevenwilson #indie #alternative 
This here is a review of 'Hand. Cannot. Erase.' by Steven Wilson, written by @maybeshivangi.  I met 'Hand. Cannot. Erase.' in January 2017 in a hostel in another city. I say met because I only heard a familiar voice (ref: Lazarus) but very differently compelling lyrics in the background while having breakfast with fifteen strangers before I complimented the person for his playlist who was later kind enough to let me go through it, which is when I was introduced to the album. I say met also because listening to this album is like meeting a part of so many people you already know, including yourself. 
Wilson wrote 'Hand. Cannot. Erase.' taking inspiration from the case of Joyce Carol Vincent, a woman who died in her north London apartment and wasn't missed by anyone for three years. contrary to what we may think, Carol was an outgoing and popular woman. Wilson captures the isolation of our modern lives in this album which is nothing short of an epic. it explores ideas of alienation in cities, nostalgia of childhood, loneliness, absurdity of the 21st century, internet and so on. some tracks will hit you like a story by Kafka would. or if you're a Radiohead fan, you'll get hints of 'OK Computer' too. each song, as is the case for any progressive rock track, is on an average 10-12 minutes long which is perfect because these songs are literally stories and they need time to be recited and you need time to take them in. 
Wilson's protagonist is a woman and he has written the tracks from her perspective; they range from internal monologues to letters to diary entries, anything that is an explosion of her thoughts which is probably what inspired the album art too. Katherine Jenkins from Wales and Ninet Tayeb from Israel have contributed in two tracks where the voice of the woman was literally required. instead of reading more while I harp about the beauty of this album, you can just go and listen to it and enjoy (includes crying) the rollercoaster that is 'Hand. Cannot. Erase.'
and while you're at it, also listen to 'The Raven That Refused to Sing (And Other Stories)'

'This Album Changed My Life' #2
We're back, with a superbly written review by @baker_street_regular, for the album Michigan by Sufjan Stevens.

Why do I love Sufjan Stevens' Michigan? Let me count the ways. 
Entering this album, is like finding yourself amidst an extended family assembled in a house gathering, a slew of siblings, every song a personality. There are sombre songs, parental in their sense of sacrifice and sadness ("For the Widows in Paradise") ; there are elaborate tunes, a group of mischievous cousins, experimental pranksters changing time signatures at whim ("All Good Naysayers"); there are silent hipsters and nerds, deep into crosswords and cubes ("Tahquamenon Falls"); there are bouncy teens, a spring in their step, a fizz of youth ("Upper Peninsula") and there are jilted lovers, aching like violin strings ("Romulus") there are optimistic uncles ("Say Yes! To Michigan), and jobless youth returned home, embarrassed in a gathering (Flint, For the Unemployed and Underpaid). Before he hit his crescendo with Chicago, here is Sufjan in all his brilliant breadth. Sure, it's a loose, baggy album, anything goes. But the range, musical and emotional, in it's mellowness, never fails me. The way he composes all these little dollops, one always feels rocking and swaying in the folds of time, and he never does it too much. You feel in safe and secure hands, you will never fall into chaos. Its like he sees all the possible routes the song can take in slo-mo, plucks it out of thin air, and carefully combines and selects it to the great joy of the one who listens. You can sometimes predict some little thing before it actually appears, a little turn of melody, a sweet lyric and there's no beauty like seeing that prophecy fulfilled. 
The portrait of this artist is incomplete without this album. He's a great host to every guest with an earphone and a ear. When you enter Sufjan's house, you get his best wine, the best seat in the house, the best attention. 
Stevens is an effing genius. Repeat till the page is full, printer.
#alternative #folk #indie #indiefolk #sufjan #asthmatickitty #sufjanstevens #alternative #instamusic #singersongwriter #acoustic #guitar

Update to let everyone know that one of my favorite musicians ever (Liz Harris aka Grouper, @grouperepuorg) is releasing a new album 'Grid of Points' on April 27th! The self-titled track is already on bandcamp for listening too. Very excited for this one. Among her past works, Grouper's album 'Dragging a Dead Deer Up A Hill' is for me, a spiritual experience. It takes minimalistic chord progressions and pairs them with excellent ambient production. There's an element of sadness but more so of comfort... Or perhaps finding comfort in melancholy. The lyrics are often obfuscated and despite having heard the album n times I still don't know the lyrics to most songs (there are none online either so good luck with that). It melds elements of folk, ambient and psychedelia. It's the album that I heard first and went 'How does she do that?!?'. It's my go to brain fog album and clears away all neurotic tendencies (temporarily but effectively :p). The way I describe it doesn't really do justice to it, because musically it's just top notch, so many elements and you discover something new each time you hear it. Will have a proper write up on it coming soon. #grouper #ambient #folk #psychedelia #indie #indiefolk #calmmusic #calm #chillout #psychedelic #piano #acoustic #musicjournalism #alternativemusic #psychedelicfolk

'This Album Changed My Life' #1
For AIR's Moon Safari, and Talkie Walkie 
Written by Anupama Nair, @puumzu
AIR literally came to me like a breath of fresh air (sorry) when I was 12 years old and stumbled upon this CD that had 5 albums by AIR burned on to it, that my sister's 12th grade best friend gave to her to listen to. I saw it lying around, got curious and played it out loud on the old Sony music player that was my lifeline since I had lost my beloved walkman. And it truly changed my life.

Moon Safari and Talkie Walkie together made me realise how incredible it is to listen to entire albums, truly a revelation for a preteen who would mostly listen to hit pop songs and some of her father's old music on shuffle. It felt like I rediscovered music.

The opening double of Moon Safari is enough to have anyone intrigued. Each song sets either a vibrant, breezy montage of videos in my mind that look just like the music sounds. From La Femme d'Argent to Sexy Boy (hilarious video; do check it out!) to All I Need (one of my favourite love songs ever) till the very last track, the music transports you to the minds of its creators. And I can never get enough of it.

The duo, Jean-Benoît Dunckel and Nicolas Godin, describe the album as "this alien, psychedelic, loungecore music you’d listen to on a Sunday morning after you’d been out clubbing the night before." Well, I'd listen to it anytime. And I strongly recommend it to you too. Off to a good start with this one, I assure you. 
#air #moonsafari #electronica #indie #instamusic #musicjournalism #musicalbum #indietronica #psychedelia #psychedelic #frenchmusic #chillout #downtempo #experimentalmusic #dreampop #alternative #alternativemusic (Link to tracks in bio!)

So I wanted to announce a new series of posts called 'This Album Changed My Life'. Aspiring writers/music enthusiasts who want to share a writeup about an album that influenced them, why it holds such a significance and a brief overview of the lyrical themes etc. are welcome to contribute. Anupama (@puumzu) will be writing the first article in the upcoming series... Expect new things soon, and follow the page for more! (Pictured is my favorite album of all time, Elliott Smith by Elliott Smith. A writeup on this is ongoing, but tough to do justice to because of the sheer power of this record, and the often brilliant lyrics and lyrical themes. It should be up in a while nonetheless)

Deerhunter Appreciation Post #2
"There's nothing I do in my life that's not stream of conscious. There's never been an interview where I didn't use that phrase at least 25 times, because people always want to know why I do things. They always want to know the story behind something, and it's like, I don't know. When I do laundry I do it stream of conscious. I don't even know what I'm doing, I just pick up random objects. Sometimes I wash candles accidentally." -Bradford Cox, Deerhunter 
#shoegaze #indie #indierock #quotes #streamofconsciousness #instamusic #rock #noise #experimental #instaquotes #indiemusic #dreampop

Pictured are the band Deerhunter. I feel like the picture does a good job of representing the band - there's a lot of dreamy night-like qualities to their music but there's also shades of grey, lyrics dealing with loneliness and isolation. The music is transcendental at times... What differentiates them from other dream pop bands is their willingness to experiment, while maintaining a strong melodic sense. Singer and composer Bradford Cox has an ear for catchy melodies. Couple that with intricate and painstakingly handled production and you have albums that are often as stunning as they can get eerie. 
Excerpt from an article - 'As a teenager, he (Bradford) dropped out of high school and his parents divorced, leaving him "to live in my childhood home alone. I literally lived in this large suburban house by myself."
With no friends, Cox became interested in how music could sound "heartbreaking or nostalgic or melancholy".'
The lyrics are often about finding comfort in isolation, with the self. The production is cozy, comforting and psychedelic. The nostalgia is reflected too... In 'Walkabout' from his solo project he sings 'What did you want to see, what did you want to be when you grew up?' in a repetitive fashion, driving home the point as the music gets more and more ethereal. The repetitive element is handled so well in other songs too (the refrain 'to get older still' in the song Little Kids, for example). The lyrics often make one crave for the times past, for the time when responsibilities were few and there was so much to explore, to discover; also a time of confusion and being overwhelmed at times. To conclude, if you could relate to themes I talked about here, you definitely will enjoy their music! Link to the music in a playlist in the bio. #deerhunter #bradfordcox #dreampop #ethereal #rock #indie #indierock #shoegaze #dreamy #instamusic #music #experimental #psychedelic #psychedelia #psychedelicrock

Video courtesy Straight Records.
'Long afloat on shipless oceans
I did all my best to smile
'Til your singing eyes and fingers
Drew me loving to your isle
And you sang
Sail to me
Let me enfold you
Here I am
Waiting to hold you' - Song to The Siren, Tim Buckley (recording from 1970)
Tim was a psychedelic folk musician with a guitar sound that I've grown to love - there's an otherworldliness to the tone he maintains. Calling him a folk musician is perhaps a disservice to him - he has experimented with jazz, psychedelia and funk on various albums. Tim's life was short and brilliant - he died at 28 from a heroin overdose, but by then he had released nine studio albums, a lot of which were immense works of love and genius that stand the test of time. #timbuckley #folk #psychedelic #psychedelia #indiefolk #instamusic #acoustic

'A heart that's full up like a landfill
A job that slowly kills you
Bruises that won't heal

You look so tired, unhappy 
Bring down the government
They don't, they don't speak for us' - No Surprises, Radiohead

The quietness of the music and the lyrics really speaks to me. Radiohead are one of those artists that believe in 'less is more', and that often shows in their minimalistic approach to music. The lyrics often involve scattered phrases that complement each other to create a mood, as compared to a traditional songwriting narrative where lyrics have an order and often send a message. This video is from an acoustic concert in Paris, back in 2003. The collective gasp at the starting of the music is just wonderful... It truly would've been a joy to witness this live. #radiohead #instamusic #indie #indierock #thomyorke #acoustic #melancholy #france #britishmusic #rock #liveinparis #piano

Album Appreciation Post #1

Carrie and Lowell by Sufjan Stevens

Carrie and Lowell was written following the demise of Carrie, Sufjans mother.
So Sufjan said in an interview how the songs were essentially therapy for him; while writing songs, he'd write down everything he was feeling at the time honestly. 
And so he goes from anger to regret to confusion (directed towards his mother, who had left him when he was young), to forgiveness, grief, and acceptance. He'll talk about the things that make life worth living and the beauty in nature and people he didn't appreciate, but not before contemplating whether he has a reason to live.
It is, strangely enough, life affirming. But not before it takes you through a rollercoaster of emotions. (Again, link to playlist in the bio in the description!) #tbt #throwback #folk #indie #country #sufjanstevens #instamusic #indiefolk

My Bloody Valentine, 1988. Vocalist Bilinda Butcher often mentions how she would intentionally be made to record her vocals right after she had woken up, during the making of album Loveless. This provided a sleepy quality to the vocals that would beautifully complement the hazy and blissed out soundscapes that guitarist Kevin Shields created, seemingly out of thin air. #tbt #throwback #shoegaze #noise #mybloodyvalentine #music #instamusic (tbh the band would probably disapprove of my liberal use of hashtags)
Link to their music in the bio!

So a writeup on the original Loveless album by My Bloody Valentine will follow, but for now I wanted to mention the excellent cover album Blue Loveless, recorded entirely by Korean artists. Check it out if you enjoy space-y blissed out music! (When You Sleep by Jowall is a personal favorite, link in the bio!) #loveless #shoegaze #koreanmusic #mybloodyvalentine #mixtapes #psychedelic #noise
